unmanned.connText:
Shared battlegrounds: nearly every domestic name is chasing the DAWG / Drone Dominance Program (Gauntlet I-II); Blue UAS/NDAA rules foreclose DJI/Chinese platforms - the structural tailwind under most theses. CCA: Increment 1 went to Anduril + General Atomics; USMC CCA = Northrop (lead) + Kratos (Valkyrie) + RTX/Pratt engine.

Collaborations: Red Cat ↔ Palantir (VNav/Warp Speed); Draganfly ↔ Palladyne (SwarmOS) & F4 Defense/DelMar; Northrop ↔ Kratos ↔ RTX (USMC CCA).
Competition: Red Cat vs. Skydio on SRR (now apparently dual-sourced - rivals AND co-suppliers); AeroVironment vs. Anduril vs. Red Cat across small-UAS/loitering; Kratos vs. Anduril/GA in the loyal-wingman tier.
Supply-chain: Unusual Machines is the clearest DJI-ban beneficiary (NDAA-compliant components into the OEMs above); Red Cat carries Chinese-component risk per short-sellers. The whole bull case rests on DJI's Covered-List exclusion redirecting demand to domestic makers.
M&A / capital: Anduril $5B raise (May-26, ~$61B, IPO pending); Ondas bought Mistral + World View; ZenaTech aggressive DaaS roll-up; UMAC buying Upgrade Energy + Rotor Lab; AVAV BlueHalo integration.
Short-seller focus: Red Cat (Kerrisdale, Fuzzy Panda); quality-of-earnings scrutiny on UMAC/ONDS/ZENA (gains/acquisition-driven) and AgEagle (distressed).

PDYN.bull: Differentiated embedded-AI autonomy software with steep FY26 guided growth and rising defense backlog.
PDYN.bear: Very early commercial stage, tiny revenue, persistent burn against ~$44M cash; lofty multiple.
PDYN.cat: Convert pilots into guided $24–27M FY26 revenue; Army SwarmOS/Gremlin-X.
PDYN.conns: Autonomy software spanning drones & ground robots; integrator ties (e.g., Draganfly).
PDYN.plain: The odd one out — it sells software, not hardware . Its AI lets drones and robots observe, learn, reason, and act on their own , including controlling swarms (SwarmOS / Palladyne Pilot). It aims to be the autonomy "brain" that plugs into other people's drones. Partnering with Israel's IAI to bring autonomy to American-made loitering munitions; demonstrated swarm autonomy in a live Army exercise.
PDYN.futuretext: If you believe the future of warfare is autonomous swarms , the most leveraged bet is owning the software layer that makes any drone autonomous — and that's exactly PDYN's pitch. Software margins could be spectacular and the TAM spans every drone and robot. This is the highest theoretical upside on the list. The reason it's Tier 4 anyway: revenue is minimal, cash is burning, and the customers (primes) are also the competitors. Pure optionality.
fchips: TAM: autonomy software across drones + robotics
Vision: the "OS" for autonomous machines
Watch: design-wins, cash runway
PDYN.take: The lottery-ticket-with-a-thesis. The idea — be the autonomy brain for everyone's drones — is the most leveraged way to bet on where warfare is heading, with potentially spectacular margins if adopted. But "if" is doing enormous work. Small, speculative position only, sized so a total loss wouldn't hurt.

KTOS.implied: At ~$10.5B on ~$1.73B revenue (~6x sales) with ~25% gross margins and only adjusted profitability, the market is paying a growth-story premium far above legacy defense primes (~1.5-2.5x sales). The price implicitly assumes revenue compounds toward ~$3B+ over the next several years as Valkyrie scales to ~40/yr and hypersonics reach ~$700M+, with margins expanding. In effect, several years of ~20%+ growth and successful CCA wins are already in the stock.

KTOS.plain: Builds jet-powered drones — the stealthy XQ-58 Valkyrie "loyal wingman" (a CCA that flies alongside fighters) — and, crucially, the jet engines that power cheap drones and missiles. Plus target drones, hypersonics, rocket systems, and military space. A defense-tech conglomerate with a genuine bottleneck asset.
KTOS.futuretext: This is the biggest "future-potential" story in Tier 1. If the U.S. really builds thousands of attritable combat drones, two things must scale: the airframes and the engines. Kratos is one of very few that can supply both. Plans call for ramping Valkyrie output and producing thousands of turbojet engines a year. If that vision lands, today's ~6× sales looks cheap in hindsight; if CCA volumes disappoint, it looks expensive. This is the clearest case where "priced for exponential" might actually be right.
fchips: TAM: CCA + drone/missile engines, potentially $10s of B
Vision: the engine + airframe supplier for mass drone warfare
Watch: Valkyrie unit ramp, engine volume
KTOS.take: The highest-quality pure-ish drone bet — the engine business is a real moat. The drop from ~$134 gives a better entry, but "cheaper than before" isn't "cheap." Core-to-aggressive for someone who believes the loyal-wingman future.

NOC.plain: One of the five big U.S. defense primes. In drones, Northrop builds the RQ-4 Global Hawk and Navy MQ-4C Triton — giant, high-altitude spy drones that loiter for 24+ hours — and it's the prime integrator on the Valkyrie CCA program (the loyal-wingman effort). It also makes the B-21 stealth bomber, missiles, and space systems. Drones are a real but small slice of a ~$42B business.
NOC.futuretext: The long game is the shift from a few exquisite $100M+ drones toward networks of cheaper collaborative aircraft — and Northrop wants to be the integrator that stitches them into crewed missions. That's a durable, decades-long role, but it's incremental growth on a giant base, not a moonshot.
fchips: TAM: ISR + CCA integration, tens of $B
Vision: the "quarterback" of manned-unmanned teaming
Pace: steady, not explosive
NOC.take: NOC is "drone exposure without the drama." If your brother wants the theme inside a portfolio anchor that pays a dividend and won't crater on one bad headline, this is it. The trade-off is obvious: drones move maybe a few percent of revenue, so the upside is muted. A ballast position, not a thesis trade.

AVAV.plain: The closest thing to an established drone-first contractor. It makes the Switchblade — a backpack "kamikaze" drone (loitering munition) — plus recon drones like Puma. In 2025 it bought BlueHalo , adding laser (directed-energy) weapons, space comms, and counter-drone. Now spans air, land, sea, space, and cyber.
AVAV.futuretext: AVAV's bet is that loitering munitions become standard-issue at scale and that directed-energy finally matures into deployable systems. If both land, it has more shots on goal than any peer. Forward valuation (~3.7× sales) is reasonable if the BlueHalo legs grow; the risk is paying for synergies that arrive slowly.

AVAV.take: The anchor if you want drone exposure without betting the farm. The only drone-first name big enough to absorb a bad quarter, and forward valuation isn't crazy. The catch: you're now also buying lasers and space, and the deal must pay off. Core holding, not a moonshot.

RCAT.implied: At ~$1.7B on ~$41M trailing sales, the market prices RCAT at ~41x sales for a cash-burning ~20% gross-margin hardware maker. To justify even a generous ~8x forward sales the company needs ~$210M revenue (~5x current), roughly the full-rate SRR program executing in full plus continued ~150%+ growth. The cap effectively assumes SRR FRP is awarded near the ~$220M figure AND Red Cat retains primary share in a confirmed multi-vendor program.

RCAT.plain: Flagship is the Black Widow , a small backpack recon drone with built-in AI. Its big moment: winning the U.S. Army's Short-Range Reconnaissance (SRR) program — beating Skydio — to become the Army's standard soldier-carried scout drone. Via its Teal Drones unit it's NDAA-compliant (China-free), exactly what the Pentagon mandates. The purest "the Army picked us" story here.
RCAT.futuretext: The upside math is simple and large: if SRR moves to full-rate production and the Army (plus allies) buys tens of thousands of Black Widows over years, today's ~$41M revenue is a rounding error vs. where it could go — and the program-of-record status makes that recurring. That's the exponential the price is reaching for. The catch: a single program means single-point-of-failure risk, and the short thesis directly attacks the size of that program.
fchips: TAM: soldier-borne recon drones, tens of thousands of units
Vision: the Army's standard scout drone franchise
Watch: full-rate production decision
RCAT.take: Cleanest "why this company wins" story — the Army chose its drone — with real franchise potential if deliveries scale. But it carries baggage: a public short campaign and a big raise. Reward if SRR becomes a multi-year, tens-of-thousands franchise is large; risk if the short critiques prove right is equally real. High-conviction-or-skip — read the short report first.

UMAC.plain: Instead of building drones, it makes the parts inside them — especially motors , plus FPV components (owns Fat Shark & Rotor Riot). The thesis: as the U.S. bans Chinese parts, someone must supply NDAA-compliant, American-made components . UMAC is building an Orlando motor factory to be that supplier — a potential "Intel moment" for domestic drone hardware. Acquiring a battery firm (Upgrade Energy) to widen the catalog.
UMAC.futuretext: The most leveraged way to bet on re-shoring the drone supply chain: if dozens of U.S. drone programs all need China-free motors and batteries, the supplier to all of them is a tollbooth on the whole industry. That's a genuinely big TAM and would justify a lot. The honest counterpoint: today it's ~$30M of revenue, the recent profit was an investment fluke, and components historically become commodities. The "exponential" here is more plausible than proven .
fchips: TAM: U.S. drone components — broad, policy-driven
Vision: the domestic "Intel inside" of drones
Watch: factory output, OEM design-wins
UMAC.take: The cleverest idea on the list, with the cash to build it. But be honest about the math: ~$1B of value on ~$30M of revenue, with a "profit" that came from investments. A venture-style bet dressed as a stock. Own only with money you can watch swing 30% in a week.

ZENA.cat: Path to 25 acquisitions by mid-2026; ZenaDrone military traction.
ZENA.plain: A micro-cap pursuing two things at once: (1) a "Drone-as-a-Service" roll-up — buying up small businesses (e.g., land-survey firms) and bolting drones onto them — which is driving eye-popping (but acquisition-fueled) revenue growth; and (2) building the ZenaDrone 2000 , a counter-UAS interceptor aimed at the ~$20B counter-drone market, with first flight tests targeted. It also waves at AI and "quantum." A lot of story for a ~$1.40 stock.
ZENA.futuretext: The bull dream is that the DaaS roll-up creates a recurring-revenue base while the ZenaDrone 2000 interceptor lands even a sliver of the counter-UAS market — either alone could re-rate a $130M company many times over. That's the lottery upside. The sober read: roll-ups financed by share issuance often destroy per-share value, the interceptor hasn't flown, and margins are deeply negative. Highest variance on the list.
fchips: TAM: drone services + C-UAS, large
Vision: services cash-cow funding a defense product
Watch: interceptor flight tests, dilution pace
ZENA.take: The most speculative name here. The growth headline (+640%) is almost entirely acquisitions paid for with stock, and the exciting part (the interceptor) hasn't flown. Real lottery-ticket optionality, but the base rate for penny-stock roll-ups is poor. If owned at all, a tiny "fun money" position — and watch the share count like a hawk.

EH.cat: CAAC public ticketed passenger-flight launch (timing TBD) — the revenue inflection.
EH.conns: CAAC regulator gates the catalyst clock; adjacent eVTOL, distinct demand story.
EH.plain: The leading passenger-drone / eVTOL pure-play — autonomous electric aircraft that carry people (think pilotless air taxis), for urban transport, tourism, and emergency use. Its EH216-S was the world's first pilotless passenger eVTOL to win full type, production, and airworthiness certificates (from China's aviation regulator), and it's flying commercial operations there. This is "drones" in the air-mobility sense, not military.
EH.futuretext: The biggest TAM on the entire list — moving people through the air autonomously — and EHang is the only name with the certifications to actually sell it today. If air taxis become normal in Chinese cities and then beyond, this is a different-magnitude opportunity. But "if" is enormous and far off: adoption, safety culture, infrastructure, and China-specific risks all gate it. This is a long-dated option on a future that may take a decade.
fchips: TAM: urban air mobility — potentially huge
Vision: autonomous passenger flight at city scale
Watch: delivery volume, route approvals
EH.take: The wild card — and the one name that's really a different theme (passenger air mobility, not defense). The upside is the largest here if urban air taxis happen; the timeline and China risk make it a long-dated bet. Only as a small "future of flight" speculation, separate from the defense names — don't confuse it with them.

ONDS.implied: At ~$4.8B on ~$51M trailing sales, ONDS trades near ~65x sales pricing in the full >=$390M FY26 guide AND continued hypergrowth beyond it. Even hitting $390M leaves the stock at ~12x forward sales for a ~20% gross-margin, cash-burning, acquisition-built business; to look reasonable at ~6x sales it needs ~$800M revenue, i.e. it must roughly double again past the already-aggressive 2026 target. The market is underwriting ~670% growth with minimal slippage and is not yet pricing integration or dilution risk.

ONDS.plain: Two-plus drone businesses. Iron Drone Raider is a counter-drone interceptor that uses radar + computer vision to chase and net hostile drones. Optimus is an autonomous "drone-in-a-box" for 24/7 inspection/security (on the Pentagon's Blue UAS list). In 2026 it went on an acquisition spree — reportedly six deals including Mistral , a defense prime with a ~$982M Army loitering-munitions program — pushing it into the attack-drone business too.
fchips: TAM: counter-UAS + munitions, $10s of B

ONDS.take: Best theme on the list, and the backlog jump genuinely strengthens the bull case I underrated before — trailing P/S really is the wrong lens here. But "the growth is real" and "the stock is expensive" can both be true: a lot of upside is already priced, and acquisitions dilute. A high-conviction thematic bet — size it like a speculation, watch the share count.

KRKNF.plain: Drones don't only fly. Kraken (Canadian) makes underwater (subsea) robots , high-resolution sonar , and — cleverly — the pressure-tolerant batteries that power other people's underwater vehicles (a picks-and-shovels angle). Uses span naval mine-hunting, seabed mapping, and protecting undersea cables/pipelines. Crucially, it's one of the few names here that is actually profitable .
KRKNF.futuretext: The under-the-radar idea: as nations wake up to how exposed undersea cables and pipelines are, subsea drones and sonar go from niche to strategic. Kraken is already profitable in that niche and consolidating it (Covelya). This is a "future potential" story where the financials already work — you're betting on a growing pie, not a turnaround. The cleanest profitable-grower on the list outside the primes.
fchips: TAM: subsea defense + offshore, multi-$B and expanding
Vision: the subsea-robotics + sensing leader
Watch: Covelya integration, naval orders
KRKNF.take: My favorite of the new additions for a quality-minded investor. It's the only small-cap here that's already profitable and growing fast , in a domain (undersea) the others ignore entirely — useful diversification away from the crowded flying-drone trade. The frictions are real (OTC listing, big acquisition). A strong "growth specialist" position — different risks than the rest, which is the point.

AVEX.plain: A full-stack defense-drone company : builds the aircraft, sensors, software, and the intelligence/mission services around them. Best known for the Phoenix Ghost one-way attack drones used in Ukraine. "Full stack" means it sells the whole ISR workflow, not just airframes — stickier and higher-margin. Public only since April 2026.
AVEX.futuretext: The bet: convert that $8.1B pipeline into booked revenue while the full-stack/ISR mix lifts margins above a typical hardware maker. If even a fraction of the pipeline lands, ~$610M of revenue could be an early waypoint, not a ceiling — and at ~3× sales the stock isn't pricing that in. The risk is government concentration: one budget cycle can stall the whole curve.

AVEX.take: The best "growth at a reasonable price" name here: ~$600M of fast-growing, combat-proven revenue at a low multiple, discounted for being a fresh IPO with gov concentration and dilution. If the next two or three reports are clean, the discount can close. My highest-conviction speculative pick — for someone okay with a young public company.

people: CEO, SpaceX - controls launch + Starlink; a 2026 IPO at ~$1.5T would anchor the sector
Founder/CEO, Rocket Lab - driving Neutron and the end-to-end strategy that defines the #2 launcher
Founder/CEO, AST SpaceMobile - betting on space-based direct-to-cell with AT&T/Verizon/Vodafone
Co-founder/CEO, Intuitive Machines - leading commercial lunar landings + the Lanteris acquisition
Founder, Anduril - $61B defense-tech disruptor expanding into space and Golden Dome
Founder, Blue Origin - New Glenn + BE-4 engines (a chokepoint) and Blue Moon lander
space.connText: The sector orbits SpaceX: Falcon/Starship lift nearly every constellation (Planet, Globalstar, AST rideshares, Kuiper, even competitors), while Starlink is simultaneously the dominant broadband threat to SES, Eutelsat/OneWeb, Viasat and Telesat, and a D2D rival to AST SpaceMobile, Globalstar (Apple) and Iridium (Stardust). A confidential ~$1.5T SpaceX IPO filed Apr 2026 would reprice the group and crystallize EchoStar (SATS), which swapped spectrum for a ~2pct SpaceX stake in a ~$40B AT&T/SpaceX deal. Propulsion is a chokepoint: Blue Origin BE-4 engines power its New Glenn and ULA Vulcan, while Ursa Major and Karman feed missiles and launchers amid a solid-motor shortage. Consolidation reshaped the map: Lockheed absorbed Terran Orbital; Rocket Lab bought laser-comms maker Mynaric; Redwire added Edge Autonomy drones; SES merged Intelsat; Maxar split into Vantor (intel/EO, Advent) and Lanteris (sat systems, now partly held by Intuitive Machines). Golden Dome is the defining defense catalyst, pulling in primes (LMT architect, NOC and L3Harris on missile-warning sats, RTX on interceptors/sensors), software (Palantir for battle management, Anduril for autonomy/C2), and components (Karman, CesiumAstro). Artemis links NASA CLPS landers (Intuitive Machines, Firefly, Astrobotic), Blue Origin Blue Moon, and the commercial-station race (Voyager Starlab with Airbus/MDA/Palantir/Northrop, plus Vast, Sierra and Axiom) to succeed the ISS. Everyone needs launch (SpaceX, then Blue Origin/Rocket Lab/Firefly), buses (MDA, Apex, K2, Astranis), components (Karman, CesiumAstro, Rocket Lab/Mynaric), and increasingly an AI/data layer (Palantir).

NOC.plain: One of the five big U.S. defense primes. In drones, Northrop builds the RQ-4 Global Hawk and Navy MQ-4C Triton — giant, high-altitude spy drones that loiter for 24+ hours — and it's the prime integrator on the Valkyrie CCA program (the loyal-wingman effort). It also makes the B-21 stealth bomber, missiles, and space systems. Drones are a real but small slice of a ~$42B business.
NOC.futuretext: The long game is the shift from a few exquisite $100M+ drones toward networks of cheaper collaborative aircraft — and Northrop wants to be the integrator that stitches them into crewed missions. That's a durable, decades-long role, but it's incremental growth on a giant base, not a moonshot.
fchips: TAM: ISR + CCA integration, tens of $B
Vision: the "quarterback" of manned-unmanned teaming
Pace: steady, not explosive
NOC.take: NOC is "drone exposure without the drama." If your brother wants the theme inside a portfolio anchor that pays a dividend and won't crater on one bad headline, this is it. The trade-off is obvious: drones move maybe a few percent of revenue, so the upside is muted. A ballast position, not a thesis trade.

LHX.conns: Owns Aerojet; SDA missile-warning; Golden Dome
LHX.plain: A large defense-tech prime focused on communications, sensors, and electronic warfare. Its drone angle is the defensive one: counter-drone systems . It won a U.S. Army order (up to ~$106M) for VAMPIRE counter-UAS launchers, turned its widely-fielded Falcon tactical radios into drone-detecting sensors (Wraith Shield — a software upgrade to 100,000+ radios already in the field), and partnered with Skydagger on drone interceptors. So as everyone buys attack drones, LHX sells the shield.
LHX.futuretext: Counter-drone may be the most under-appreciated growth wedge in defense: drone attacks have outpaced the defenses against them, and militaries are now scrambling to catch up. LHX's edge is that it can field the shield through hardware already in soldiers' hands. The size of the prize is large; the question is what share LHX captures and at what margin.
fchips: TAM: counter-UAS est. ~$20B+ and growing
Vision: layered drone defense across the force
Pace: steady-to-fast in the C-UAS slice
LHX.take: If your brother believes the next phase of drone warfare is about defending against swarms (a very reasonable view), LHX is the scaled, profitable way to own that without micro-cap risk. Same caveat as NOC: it's a slice of a giant. A lower-risk satellite position, paired with one pure-play.

SPACEX.note: Dominant launch (Falcon/Starship) + Starlink broadband/D2D. Confidential IPO filed Apr 2026, ~$1.5T target. The center of the sector; most names depend on it for launch.
SPACEX.cash: Self-funding via Starlink cash flow + periodic tender offers
SPACEX.bull: Dominant global launch monopoly plus a Starlink subscriber base scaling toward broadband and direct-to-cell, with Starship unlocking radically lower cost to orbit.
SPACEX.bear: A ~$400B valuation prices in flawless Starship execution and Starlink margins while keeping the company hostage to Musk key-man and regulatory risk.
SPACEX.valnote: Implied ~$400B from late-2025/early-2026 tender offers; est as-of Jun 2026

BLUEORIGIN.note: New Glenn heavy-lift + BE-4 engines (also power ULA Vulcan). Blue Moon lunar lander. Bezos-funded; no IPO on calendar.
BLUEORIGIN.cash: Funded by Bezos (~$1B+/yr) plus growing NASA/DoD and BE-4 engine contracts
BLUEORIGIN.bull: New Glenn now flying, BE-4 engines sell to ULA, and Blue Moon lunar plus Orbital Reef station give multiple government-anchored revenue legs backed by Bezos's deep pockets.
BLUEORIGIN.bear: Years behind SpaceX on cadence and reuse, chronically slow execution, and still deeply unprofitable despite enormous cumulative investment.
BLUEORIGIN.valnote: Est private valuation; no public raise, Bezos-funded; as-of Jun 2026

STOKE.note: Developing fully reusable rocket (Nova). ~$1B+ raised. National-security backed.
STOKE.cash: Series C (~$260M+ raised cumulatively); well-capitalized for Nova development
STOKE.bull: Novel full-flow regen-cooled second stage targets true 100% reusability, the holy grail that could leapfrog incumbents on cost if Nova reaches orbit.
STOKE.bear: Pre-orbital with unproven tech, years from revenue, and competing against SpaceX Starship and a crowded reusable-launch field.
STOKE.valnote: Est ~$1.5B post Series C; as-of Jun 2026

RELATIVITY.note: Terran R medium-lift, 3D-printed. Schmidt-backed recapitalization. Pre-orbital; long manifest.
RELATIVITY.cash: Recapitalized with major backing led by Eric Schmidt; well-funded
RELATIVITY.bull: Eric Schmidt's capital and leadership plus 3D-printed manufacturing aim Terran R at the medium-lift reusable gap behind Falcon 9.
RELATIVITY.bear: Pivoted away from Terran 1, still pre-orbital on Terran R, and burning heavily while SpaceX and Rocket Lab fill the same medium-lift demand.
RELATIVITY.valnote: Est valuation post Schmidt-led recap; as-of Jun 2026

ASTRA.note: Taken private 2024. Ships satellite electric-propulsion engines while developing Rocket 4.
ASTRA.cash: Taken private by founders in 2024; thinly capitalized, relies on propulsion contracts
ASTRA.bull: Astra Spacecraft Engine electric propulsion has a real order backlog that could anchor a leaner private company post-delisting.
ASTRA.bear: Repeated launch failures gutted the launch business, and the company remains cash-strapped with a tiny revenue base and weak balance sheet.
ASTRA.valnote: Est small private value post 2024 take-private; as-of Jun 2026

ASTRANIS.note: Dedicated small MicroGEO comm sats for regional/sovereign connectivity. Andreessen/BlackRock backed.
ASTRANIS.cash: Series D (~$200M+) plus large debt facility; well-funded
ASTRANIS.bull: Small dedicated GEO comms satellites let Astranis sell capacity to underserved regions and governments at attractive economics versus mega-constellations.
ASTRANIS.bear: On-orbit anomalies, capital-intensive satellite builds, and competition from LEO broadband threaten the dedicated-GEO model's returns.
ASTRANIS.valnote: Est ~$4B post Series D / debt; as-of Jun 2026

APEX.note: Productized satellite buses (Aries/Nova/Comet). Series D mid-2025 ~$2.3B. Defense/commercial demand.
APEX.cash: Series C (~$200M raised cumulatively); strong backlog and funding
APEX.bull: Productized, configurable satellite buses (Aries/Nova/Comet) ride the booming defense and commercial constellation demand with fast delivery times.
APEX.bear: Bus manufacturing is becoming crowded, margins are unproven at scale, and revenue depends on lumpy government and constellation orders.
APEX.valnote: Est ~$1.3B post Series C; as-of Jun 2026

K2.note: Mega-class high-power buses for defense/Golden Dome-scale missions. Fast-growing.
K2.cash: Series B (~$110M+); funded for Mega-class bus development
K2.bull: Large, high-power Mega-class buses built for cheap Starship-era mass-to-orbit position K2 for power-hungry defense and Golden Dome payloads.
K2.bear: Thesis depends on low-cost heavy lift arriving on schedule, and K2 is early with minimal flight heritage against established bus makers.
K2.valnote: Est ~$1.5B post Series B; as-of Jun 2026

ICEYE.note: Leading commercial SAR (radar) imaging constellation. Strong defense/intel + insurance demand.
ICEYE.cash: Series D+ extensions; strong recurring data and government revenue
ICEYE.bull: The largest SAR constellation gives ICEYE all-weather, day-night imaging with deep defense, insurance, and government contracts and a near-profitable model.
ICEYE.bear: Capital-intensive constellation refresh and intensifying SAR competition pressure pricing as governments build sovereign capacity.

UMBRA.note: Very high-resolution commercial SAR. US defense/intel customers; competes ICEYE, Capella.
UMBRA.cash: Privately funded; growing defense and intelligence contracts
UMBRA.bull: Highest-resolution commercial SAR (down to 16cm) plus open data licensing wins fast-growing US defense and intelligence demand.
UMBRA.bear: Smaller constellation than ICEYE, heavy reliance on lumpy government contracts, and a capital-hungry path to a fully scaled fleet.

VANTOR.note: Renamed from Maxar Intelligence (2025 split). WorldView Legion optical; NRO anchor. Lanteris is the sister sat-systems arm (now partly held by Intuitive Machines).
VANTOR.cash: Carved out from Maxar (Advent-owned); cash-generative but debt-laden
VANTOR.bull: Rebranded Maxar Intelligence owns premier high-res optical imagery and entrenched NRO/government contracts with strong recurring margins.
VANTOR.bear: Heavy LBO debt, slow growth, and rising competition from cheaper SAR and proliferated optical constellations cap upside.
VANTOR.valnote: Est segment value of ex-Maxar Intelligence under Advent; as-of Jun 2026

VAST.note: Haven-1 first commercial station targeting launch; pursuing ISS-successor role. Competes Starlab, Axiom, Sierra.
VAST.cash: Self-funded by Jed McCaleb (~$300M+ committed); building Haven-1
VAST.bull: Haven-1 targets being the first commercial space station in orbit, giving Vast first-mover position for the post-ISS LEO economy and NASA's CLD program.
VAST.bear: Pre-revenue, dependent on founder funding and a single launch slot, with station demand and NASA budgets uncertain.
VAST.valnote: Est private value, McCaleb-funded; as-of Jun 2026

SIERRA.note: Dream Chaser cargo spaceplane; LIFE inflatable habitats (Orbital Reef). Defense + commercial LEO.
SIERRA.bull: Dream Chaser runway-landing spaceplane plus inflatable LIFE habitat give Sierra unique reusable cargo and station hardware for NASA and commercial customers.
SIERRA.bear: Dream Chaser is years late, the company burns cash, and its 2021 valuation looks stretched amid repeated delays.
SIERRA.valnote: Est ~$5.3B carried from 2021 round; likely stale; as-of Jun 2026

AXIOM.note: Private astronaut missions; commercial station modules; NASA xEVAS spacesuits. Funding pressure noted 2025.
AXIOM.cash: Raised ~$500M; faced liquidity pressure, restructuring/seeking funds
AXIOM.bull: Axiom runs private astronaut missions, builds the ISS-attached commercial station, and supplies Artemis spacesuits, giving it three NASA-anchored businesses.
AXIOM.bear: Recurring cash crunches, leadership turnover, and a capital-intensive station roadmap raise real solvency and execution risk.
AXIOM.valnote: Est private value; reduced from peak amid funding strain; as-of Jun 2026

ASTROBOTIC.note: NASA CLPS lunar lander provider + LunaGrid power. Competes Intuitive Machines, Firefly.
ASTROBOTIC.cash: Privately funded plus NASA CLPS task orders; modest balance sheet
ASTROBOTIC.bull: NASA CLPS contracts and lunar infrastructure (landers, rovers, power) make Astrobotic a key vendor for a growing cislunar economy.
ASTROBOTIC.bear: Peregrine's failed first landing, thin margins, and dependence on uncertain NASA lunar budgets cloud the path to profitability.

ANDURIL.note: Defense-tech leader; $61B (May 2026). Lattice C2, autonomy, growing space/Golden Dome ambitions. Likely IPO candidate.
ANDURIL.cash: Series G (~$2.5B raised) at ~$30B; very well-capitalized
ANDURIL.bull: Anduril's software-defined autonomous weapons and Lattice OS are winning large DoD programs, with Golden Dome and space sensing adding a fast-growing space leg.
ANDURIL.bear: A ~$30B valuation on early defense revenue prices in flawless program wins, and space is still a small slice of a mostly-terrestrial defense business.
ANDURIL.valnote: ~$30.5B Series G (2025); space is minority of business; as-of Jun 2026

STARLINK.note: Dominant LEO broadband + Direct-to-Cell. Primary value driver behind SpaceX IPO. Competes Kuiper, OneWeb, ASTS.
STARLINK.cash: Funded within SpaceX; self-sustaining from subscriber cash flow
STARLINK.bull: Over 6M+ subscribers, scaling direct-to-cell and enterprise/government revenue make Starlink the dominant and profitable LEO broadband network.
STARLINK.bear: Massive ongoing capex for satellite replenishment plus Kuiper and regulatory competition could compress the standalone value implied here.
STARLINK.valnote: Est standalone implied value as majority of SpaceX EV; as-of Jun 2026

KUIPER.note: Amazon LEO broadband (~3,200 sats). Funded by Amazon; direct Starlink challenger.
KUIPER.cash: Funded by Amazon (~$10B+ committed); early commercial deployment
KUIPER.bull: Amazon's balance sheet, AWS integration, and distribution give Kuiper a credible path to the #2 LEO broadband network behind Starlink.
KUIPER.bear: Years behind Starlink on satellites in orbit, enormous capex, and an uncertain standalone economic return make value highly speculative.
KUIPER.valnote: Est standalone implied value within Amazon; early deployment; as-of Jun 2026

CESIUM.note: Active phased-array antenna/comms payloads for sats, aircraft, defense. Key components supplier.
CESIUM.cash: Series C (~$130M+ raised); funded for active antenna scale-up
CESIUM.bull: Active phased-array antennas and software-defined comms payloads ride surging demand from defense and proliferated commercial constellations.
CESIUM.bear: Component/payload supplier margins are thin, customer concentration is high, and larger primes can build competing phased arrays in-house.
CESIUM.valnote: Est ~$0.9B post Series C; as-of Jun 2026

URSAMAJOR.note: Independent rocket-engine maker + solid rocket motors for missiles. Defense propulsion amid motor shortage.
URSAMAJOR.cash: Raised ~$280M+ cumulatively; funded for Hadley/Ripley/Draper engines
URSAMAJOR.bull: As an independent rocket-engine and solid-propulsion supplier, Ursa Major fills a defense-prioritized gap for missiles and launch with growing DoD demand.
URSAMAJOR.bear: Engine manufacturing is capital-intensive and low-margin, with customer concentration and competition from in-house propulsion at primes.

people: Chairman of Oklo and OpenAI CEO - personifies the AI-needs-nuclear narrative; drives Oklo sentiment
CEO of Cameco - steward of the Western uranium incumbent and the Westinghouse stake
CEO of X-energy - ex-DOE deputy leading the Amazon-backed SMR that ran the largest nuclear IPO ever
CEO of Constellation - runs the largest US nuclear fleet; struck the Microsoft TMI-restart PPA
CEO of TerraPower - leads the Gates-backed Natrium build with the first NRC non-LWR permit
CEO of Commonwealth Fusion - best-funded private fusion (SPARC/ARC) with a Google offtake
nuclear.connText: The nuclear thesis is now a power-supply thesis for AI. Hyperscalers are the demand engine: Microsoft anchored Constellations Three Mile Island restart with a 20-year PPA; Amazon both funds X-energy (Xe-100 SMRs) and buys ~1.92GW of Talen/Susquehanna nuclear for AWS; Meta signed PPAs with Vistra and a multi-gigawatt Entergy Louisiana datacenter; Google has offtake deals with Kairos and Commonwealth Fusion. Policy is the accelerant - executive orders created the DOE Reactor Pilot Program (>=3 reactors critical by Jul 4 2026, bypassing slow NRC licensing), TerraPower won the first-ever NRC construction permit for a non-light-water reactor, and Russia-supply bans push domestic sourcing. The binding constraint is HALEU: nearly every advanced reactor (Oklo, X-energy, TerraPower, Kairos) needs it, and Centrus (LEU) is the only US-owned licensed enricher, with Urenco and Orano building Western capacity - making LEU the keystone everyone depends on. Underneath, the uranium price drives miner economics, where Cameco sits at the center owning 49pct of Westinghouse (AP1000/AP300 plus a fuel-and-service annuity) with Brookfield, while US ISR names (UEC, Ur-Energy, enCore, Energy Fuels) lever the domestic-supply push. The chain runs: utilities/hyperscalers (CEG, VST, TLN + MSFT/Meta/Amazon/Google) -> reactor developers (OKLO, SMR, XE, TerraPower) -> fuel/HALEU (LEU, Westinghouse) -> miners (CCJ and peers) -> picks-and-shovels suppliers (BWXT, GEV, MIR, CW) paid on every build regardless of which design wins.

OKLO.implied: At ~$8.7B market cap with ~$0 revenue, the stock prices a successful multi-gigawatt fleet, not the first plant. A ~50 MWe Aurora at INL is a rounding error; to justify the cap, investors implicitly assume Oklo deploys on the order of 1-3 GW of operating capacity (roughly 20-60 plants) at attractive PPA economics over the next decade, with the 14 GW order book substantially converting to binding contracts. That implies ~$8.7B of value against capacity that is years from a single watt of commercial output.

RADIANT_PRIV.note: Kaleidos portable 1MW microreactor for remote/datacenter power. ~$225M raised; DOE fuel/test partnerships. Pre-revenue.

LAST_PRIV.note: PWR-20 modular plant sold as a power product; ~$32B of commercial agreements signed, UK/EU focus. Pre-revenue.

research_sector.market_size: Global UAV program spending ~$20.3B by 2025 (Teal Group), expected to more than triple over the decade; military UAS ~$216.5B cumulative spend over next decade; civil UAS $10.1B in 2024 -> $20.6B by 2033
segments: Military still dominates global UAV market; civil/commercial UAS forecast to grow faster than military over the decade
Military UAS ~$216.5B over next decade: ~$162.2B procurement + ~$72.5B R&D; US = ~81% of military R&D and ~48% of military procurement through 2030
High Altitude Long Endurance (HALE) UAVs = largest military segment, ~1/3 of decade's sales; growth led by UCAVs and HALE
Civil UAS total revenue ~$162.3B over the period at 6.8% CAGR
Worldwide commercial UAS market projected to reach $9.5B by 2028; Agriculture largest commercial sector by 2030; Delivery leading US commercial sector by 2030 (middle-mile favored over last-mile)
Industrial Inspection a key commercial driver, with Construction the largest portion
Regional: demand strongest in North America, Middle East, Asia-Pacific; China leads consumer UAS (DJI ~70% global commercial share 2024)
China low-altitude economy: 1.5T yuan (~$209.9B) in 2025 rising to 3.5T yuan by 2035; >670B yuan (~$93B) in 2024, >1T yuan (~$137B) by 2026; >1.7M registered civil UAVs in 2024
Europe drone services market could reach EUR14.5B and 145,000 jobs by 2030 (European Commission)
US grocery delivery market >$200B in 2024 -> $271B by 2028 (7.4% CAGR); drones could handle up to ~25% of US grocery deliveries by 2026 (Wing)
leaders: Skydio, Parrot, Freefly, Teal (NDAA-compliant commercial alternatives)
Helsing, Stark Defence, Perennial Autonomy (emerging defense drone startups)
takeaways: Sector seen as the most dynamic growth area in aerospace this decade; UAV spending to more than triple across military, commercial and consumer (Teal Group).
Demand splitting into three pools: battlefield autonomy, civil airspace integration, and counter-UAS (C-UAS) protection; autonomy/sensor fusion/mission software now as important as airframe.

US regulatory shift reshaping commercial supply: FCC added all foreign-made UAS to Covered List (22 Dec 2025), effectively blocking new DJI models and pushing operators to domestic NDAA-compliant platforms.

Trump's June 2025 executive orders ('Restoring American Airspace Sovereignty', 'Unleashing American Drone Dominance') aim to boost domestic production; Pentagon launched $1B 'Drone Dominance' initiative to field 300,000+ one-way attack drones by 2028.
Global military expenditure hit $2.72T in 2024 (+9.8% YoY, largest jump since 1988; SIPRI); US $997B, China ~$314B; DoD FY26 carved out $9.4B for unmanned aerial systems.

Europe regionalizing UAV supply (Elbit Romania facility, AGILE EUR115M fast-track fund); Germany to field ~8,300 UAS by 2029 and awarded EUR536M to Helsing/Stark (scalable to EUR1B each); C-UAS becoming permanent security layer (NATO BOREADES at Brussels HQ).
Market highly concentrated: ~500+ companies globally (~200 commercial, ~300 military); Drone-as-a-Service (DaaS) flagged as one of the fastest-growing commercial models.
